chunks.text: EE Courses at UC Berkeley include introductory seminars like EE 24 Freshman Seminar, which explores intellectual topics in small groups, and EE 25 What Electrical Engineers Do, where recent graduates share their experiences. EE 39 Freshman/Sophomore Seminar offers similar opportunities for lower division students. EE 42 Introduction to Digital Electronics covers basic electrical engineering concepts, circuit elements, and digital logic gates. EE 49 Electronics for the Internet of Things teaches building circuits that interact with the environment and communicate wirelessly.
chunks.question: What are some introductory EE courses at UC Berkeley?

chunks.text: Sophomore and field study courses include EE 84 Sophomore Seminar for interactive learning in small groups, EE 97 Field Study for off-campus programs, and EE 98 Directed Group Study for exploring new developments. EE 99 Individual Study allows supervised research for undergraduates with fewer than 60 units. Advanced courses like EE 105 Microelectronic Devices and Circuits cover semiconductor properties, MOSFET modeling, and amplifier design. EE C106A Introduction to Robotics introduces kinematics, dynamics, and control of robot manipulators.
chunks.question: What courses involve field study or group projects?
chunks.answer: Field study and directed group study.

chunks.text: Power and energy courses include EE 108 Introduction to Electric Power and Renewable Energy, covering electric power conversion and renewable sources. EE 113 Power Electronics focuses on power conversion circuits and magnetic devices. EE 113B Power Electronics Design emphasizes practical design and hardware implementation of converters. Electromagnetics and optics are covered in EE 117 Electromagnetic Fields and Waves, which includes Maxwell's equations and wireless technologies, and EE 118 Introduction to Optical Engineering, covering geometrical optics and diffraction.
chunks.question: Which courses focus on power electronics and renewable energy?
chunks.answer: Electric power and power electronics courses.

chunks.text: Signals and communication courses include EE 120 Signals and Systems, covering transform techniques and applications. EE 121 Introduction to Digital Communication Systems addresses digital communication principles and modulation. EE 122 Introduction to Communication Networks focuses on wired and wireless network fundamentals. EE 123 Digital Signal Processing covers discrete time signals and filter design. EE 126 Probability and Random Processes introduces probability concepts useful in networks and signal processing.
chunks.question: What topics do signals and communication courses cover?

chunks.text: Control systems and devices include EE C128 Feedback Control Systems for analysis and synthesis of linear feedback systems. EE 130 Integrated-Circuit Devices covers semiconductor properties and device fabrication. EE 134 Fundamentals of Photovoltaic Devices teaches solar cell physics and light management. Power systems are addressed in EE 137A Introduction to Electric Power Systems, focusing on the electric grid, and EE 137B, covering emerging technologies like solar and wind generation.
chunks.question: Which courses involve control systems and photovoltaic devices?
chunks.answer: Feedback control and solar cell physics.

chunks.text: Integrated circuits courses include EE 140 Linear Integrated Circuits on transistor amplifiers and operational amplifiers. EE 141 Introduction to Digital Integrated Circuits covers CMOS devices and design optimization. EE 142 Integrated Circuits for Communications focuses on circuits for wireless communication. EE 143 Microfabrication Technology teaches integrated circuit fabrication processes. Medical imaging is covered in EE C145B Medical Imaging Signals and Systems, applying linear systems to X-ray and MRI analysis.
chunks.question: What do integrated circuits courses at Berkeley cover?
chunks.answer: CMOS devices and wireless communication circuits.

chunks.text: Laboratory and specialized courses include EE C145L Introductory Electronic Transducers Laboratory for measuring physical quantities, and EE C145M Introductory Microcomputer Interfacing Laboratory for data acquisition. EE 146L Application Specific Integrated Circuits Laboratory covers ASIC design and RISC-V processor implementation. EE 147 Introduction to Microelectromechanical Systems (MEMS) teaches micromachining and sensor design. EE 156 Business and Finance for Engineers introduces business principles for engineers.
chunks.question: What laboratory courses are available in EE?
chunks.answer: Transducers, microcomputer interfacing, and ASIC labs.

chunks.text: Advanced undergraduate courses include EE 192 Mechatronic Design Laboratory for designing mechatronic systems. EE 194 Special Topics covers varying subjects each semester. EE H196A and H196B Senior Honors Thesis Research involve thesis work under faculty supervision. Field studies like EE 197 and group studies like EE 198 offer practical experience. EE 199 Supervised Independent Study allows independent research with enrollment restrictions.
chunks.question: What are some advanced project-based EE courses?
chunks.answer: Mechatronic design and honors thesis research.

chunks.text: Graduate-level EE courses include EE 206A Introduction to Robotics and EE 206B Robotic Manipulation and Interaction, covering kinematics and multi-robot systems. EE 210 Applied Electromagnetic Theory addresses boundary value problems and waveguides. Power electronics continues with EE 213A and EE 213B. EE 218A Introduction to Optical Engineering covers optical systems principles. EE 219B Logic Synthesis focuses on digital circuit optimization and verification.
chunks.question: What graduate courses cover robotics and electromagnetics?

chunks.text: Control and systems graduate courses include EE C220A Advanced Control Systems I on linear dynamic systems, and EE C220B Experiential Advanced Control Design I for SISO and MIMO controllers. EE 221A Linear System Theory covers basic system concepts and controllability. EE 222 Nonlinear Systems--Analysis, Stability and Control introduces nonlinear system analysis. EE 223 Stochastic Systems: Estimation and Control focuses on parameter estimation and adaptive control.
chunks.question: Which graduate courses deal with control and stochastic systems?

chunks.text: Communication and signal processing graduate courses include EE 224A Digital Communications on modulation and receiver design, and EE 224B Fundamentals of Wireless Communication covering multipath fading and multiple antenna systems. EE 225D Audio Signal Processing in Humans and Machines introduces speech and music processing. EE 226A Random Processes in Systems covers probability and filtering, while EE 226B addresses advanced topics like stochastic calculus.
chunks.question: What graduate courses focus on digital communications?

chunks.text: Optimization and devices graduate courses include EE 227BT Convex Optimization covering convex problems and applications. EE 230A Integrated-Circuit Devices reviews semiconductor properties. EE 230B Solid State Devices focuses on MOS field-effect transistors. EE 232 Lightwave Devices introduces optoelectronic devices like diode lasers. EE 234A Fundamentals of Photovoltaic Devices covers solar cell physics and economic considerations.
chunks.question: Which courses cover convex optimization and solid state devices?

chunks.text: Analog and digital IC graduate courses include EE 240A Analog Integrated Circuits on transistor amplifiers and feedback. EE 240B Advanced Analog Integrated Circuits covers optimized design of operational amplifiers. EE 241A Introduction to Digital Integrated Circuits addresses CMOS devices and design methodologies. EE 242A Integrated Circuits for Communications focuses on circuits for wireless systems. EE 243 Advanced IC Processing and Layout teaches fabrication processes like lithography.
chunks.question: What do analog and digital IC graduate courses involve?

chunks.text: MEMS and embedded systems graduate courses include EE 247A Introduction to Microelectromechanical Systems (MEMS) on micromachining techniques. EE C247B Introduction to MEMS Design covers physics and fabrication of MEMS. EE C249A Introduction to Embedded Systems introduces real-time embedded systems with laboratory projects. EE 290 Advanced Topics in Electrical Engineering covers current research interests in various subfields like CAD and solid state devices.
chunks.question: Which courses focus on MEMS and embedded systems?

chunks.text: EECS courses include EECS 16A Foundations of Signals, Dynamical Systems, and Information Processing, introducing signals and machine learning. EECS 16B Introduction to Circuits & Devices covers circuits and analysis methods. EECS C106A and C106B parallel EE robotics courses. EECS 126 Probability and Random Processes mirrors EE 126. EECS 149 Introduction to Embedded and Cyber Physical Systems focuses on modeling and design of embedded systems.
chunks.question: What are some foundational EECS courses?

chunks.text: CS courses include CS 61A The Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs on programming abstraction. CS 61B Data Structures covers dynamic data structures and Java. CS 61C Great Ideas of Computer Architecture addresses machine structures. CS 70 Discrete Mathematics and Probability Theory includes logic and probability. CS 170 Efficient Algorithms and Intractable Problems focuses on algorithm design and NP-completeness.
chunks.question: What do introductory CS courses cover?

chunks.text: Advanced CS courses include CS 161 Computer Security on cryptography and network security. CS 162 Operating Systems and System Programming covers OS concepts. CS 164 Programming Languages and Compilers surveys languages and compilation. CS 188 Introduction to Artificial Intelligence addresses search and machine learning. CS 189 Introduction to Machine Learning covers theoretical foundations and applications.
chunks.question: Which CS courses focus on security and AI?

chunks.text: Graduate CS courses include CS 260A User Interface Design and Development on design and evaluation. CS 261 Security in Computer Systems surveys modern security topics. CS 262A Advanced Topics in Computer Systems covers systems management. CS 270 Combinatorial Algorithms and Data Structures addresses network flow and matching. CS 276 Cryptography focuses on cryptographic theory and applications.
chunks.question: What topics do graduate CS courses cover?

chunks.text: Specialized graduate CS courses include CS 280A Intro to Computer Vision and Computational Photography on image processing. CS 282A Designing, Visualizing and Understanding Deep Neural Networks covers deep learning principles. CS 284A Foundations of Computer Graphics addresses modeling and rendering. CS 285 Deep Reinforcement Learning, Decision Making, and Control intersects control and deep learning. CS 288 Advanced Natural Language Processing surveys NLP methods and transformers.
chunks.question: Which CS courses involve vision and deep learning?
chunks.answer: Computer vision and deep neural networks.

chunks.text: Teaching and research courses include CS 302 Designing Computer Science Education on teaching techniques. CS 375 Teaching Techniques for Computer Science focuses on effective teaching for TAs. CS 399 Professional Preparation involves supervised teaching. EE 375 Teaching Techniques for Electrical Engineering orients new instructors. EE 602 and CS 602 Individual Study for Doctoral Students provide preparation for Ph.D. exams.
chunks.question: What courses prepare students for teaching roles?
